color_profiles.name: Pigeon's Blood Ruby
colors: pure-red with slight blue fluorescent overtone
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ at optimal concentration (~0.5–1.5% Cr2O3); strong red fluorescence under UV complements natural redness in daylight.

color_profiles.name: Burmese Ruby (general)
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ chromophore; trace Fe adds depth.

color_profiles.name: Thai/Cambodian Ruby
color_profiles.cause: Higher Fe content alongside Cr3+ creates darker, less fluorescent red.

color_profiles.name: Star Ruby
colors: red body with six-rayed white asterism
color_profiles.cause: Silk inclusions (rutile needles oriented along three crystallographic directions) producing asterism when cut as cabochon.

visual_notes: Ruby is the red variety of corundum — the intensity of Cr3+ fluorescence in daylight gives Burmese rubies an inner glow that red garnets and spinel cannot replicate. In strong sunlight, a fine Burmese ruby appears to emit light. The six-rayed star in star ruby is one of the most captivating optical effects in gemology.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Strong red fluorescence under UV (LW and SW) — Cr3+ emission; Burmese/marble-hosted rubies fluoresce more strongly than basalt-hosted Thai rubies (which have higher Fe quenching fluorescence)
optical_effects.asterism: Six-rayed star in star ruby — from intersecting rutile (silk) needle inclusions parallel to three directions in {0001} basal plane
